one_line_takeaway: A defence-scoped narrative survey that organizes ~80 works on LLMs in cyber defence into a seven-area taxonomy and synthesizes recurring open challenges (domain customization, dataset scarcity, real-time cost, adversarial fragility, interpretability) -- useful as an orientation map, but it contributes no new method, experiment, or dataset.
key_finding: The synthesis in the Challenges section is the paper's real payload: LLMs are already being applied across every cyber-defence sub-area, but adoption is bottlenecked by a recurring set of limits -- poor domain customization (general-purpose pretraining gives weak cyber context, and fine-tuning is constrained by scarce, sensitive labeled data), the absence of large clean cyber-defence-specific datasets, the ever-changing attack landscape that outdates models, real-time infeasibility due to heavy compute/data needs, adversarial fragility, privacy leakage, and lack of interpretability/transparency (the 'black box' problem for security analysts).
core_contribution: This is a narrative survey (not an empirical study) of how Large Language Models are being applied to CYBER DEFENCE specifically. Its own contribution is organizational rather than methodological: (1) a taxonomy that classifies LLM-based cyber-defence work into seven areas -- threat intelligence, vulnerability assessment, network security, privacy preservation, awareness and training, cyber-security operations automation, and ethical LLMs; (2) a background primer tracing the lineage from deep learning, GANs, and Transformers to BERT and GPT; and (3) a synthesis of open challenges and future research directions. The authors explicitly scope the survey to DEFENCE only, distinguishing it from prior surveys that cover all of cyber security (they cite Yao et al. [26]) or the vulnerabilities/drawbacks of LLMs themselves ([27]). It surveys close to 80 articles.
threat_model: As a survey it does not commit to one attacker model; instead it spans two directions. (a) Defensive scope -- threat classes covered by the surveyed tools: phishing/spam/spear-phishing emails and SMS, malware/ransomware and obfuscated code, network intrusions/DDoS/IDS evasion (including CAN-bus and satellite/UAV networks), cyber threat intelligence extraction, and smart-contract exploits. (b) Threats to and from the LLMs themselves, which the paper flags explicitly: adversarial/crafted-input attacks on LLM classifiers, PII/privacy leakage from training data, and offensive misuse of LLMs to develop attacks (the paper's 'double faces' framing, e.g., LLMs that both generate and detect phishing). It does not propose or evaluate a defence of its own.
limitations: Being defence-only, it deliberately excludes the offensive/threat-generation side and general LLM-security, so it is not a complete picture of LLMs in cyber security. The prose is largely descriptive summarization rather than critical, comparative analysis: it claims per-method 'strengths and weaknesses' but rarely contrasts methods head-to-head or reports comparable metrics across works. There is no systematic search methodology, so coverage completeness and selection bias cannot be assessed. The writing contains numerous typos and grammatical errors. As a 2024 survey (arXiv May 2024) it is already dated relative to fast-moving LLM security work.

quantitative_results: Survey scope (the paper's OWN figures): close to 80 articles surveyed on LLMs in cyber defence.

Taxonomy size (OWN): 7 cyber-defence categories -- threat intelligence, vulnerability assessment, network security, privacy preservation, awareness and training, operations automation, and ethical LLMs.

NOTE -- the following numbers belong to SURVEYED works, not to this paper: 204 real-world STIX-ontology CTI reports (Siracusano et al./aCTIon, ref [30], using GPT-3.5); a phishing-email dataset targeting 193 brands across 19 languages from honeypots Aug 2022-Oct 2023 (Koide et al./ChatSpamDetector, ref [86]); CIDDS-001 and CIDDS-002 network-flow datasets (Nguyen et al., ref [63]); estimated cyber-crime cost of ~$1 trillion/year for the US and ~$20 billion worldwide (cited from Sviatun/Shukla et al., ref [12]).
